examples.title: Algorithm: IoT Device Data Flow
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the fundamental data flow in an IoT device from sensor reading to communication
examples.code:
ALGORITHM IoT Device Data Collection
INPUT: Sensor type, Sampling interval, Destination endpoint
OUTPUT: Processed data sent to network

BEGIN
    INITIALIZE communication module
    INITIALIZE sensors based on sensor type
    SET timer = 0
    
    WHILE device is powered ON DO
        IF timer >= Sampling interval THEN
            // Step 1: Read sensor data
            CALL readSensor(sensorType)
            rawData = RETURN value
            
            // Step 2: Process the data
            CALL processData(rawData)
            processedData = RETURN value
            
            // Step 3: Check if action needed
            IF processedData exceeds threshold THEN
                CALL triggerActuator(processedData)
            END IF
            
            // Step 4: Transmit to network
            CALL transmitData(processedData, Destination)
            
            // Step 5: Manage power
            CALL enterLowPowerMode()
            
            RESET timer
        END IF
        
        INCREMENT timer by 1 second
        CALL enterSleepMode()
    END WHILE
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the core IoT device loop: reading sensor data at regular intervals, processing it locally, potentially triggering actuators based on thresholds, transmitting to the network, and entering power-saving sleep modes. This is the fundamental pattern used in all IoT devices.

examples.title: Python: IoT Sensor Data Collection Simulation
examples.description: Python implementation simulating an IoT device collecting and processing sensor data with power management
examples.code:
import time
import random
import json

class Sensor:
    """Base class for IoT sensors"""
    def __init__(self, name, unit):
        self.name = name
        self.unit = unit
        self.value = 0
    
    def read(self):
        """Read sensor value - to be overridden by subclasses"""
        raise NotImplementedError

class TemperatureSensor(Sensor):
    """Temperature sensor simulation (thermistor-like)"""
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__("Temperature", "°C")
    
    def read(self):
        # Simulate realistic temperature range: 15-35°C
        self.value = round(random.uniform(15.0, 35.0), 2)
        return self.value

class HumiditySensor(Sensor):
    """Humidity sensor simulation"""
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__("Humidity", "%")
    
    def read(self):
        # Simulate realistic humidity: 30-90%
        self.value = round(random.uniform(30.0, 90.0), 2)
        return self.value

class IoTDevice:
    """Simulates an IoT device with sensors, processing, and communication"""
    def __init__(self, device_id, sampling_interval=5):
        self.device_id = device_id
        self.sampling_interval = sampling_interval
        self.sensors = []
        self.is_sleeping = False
        
    def add_sensor(self, sensor):
        """Add a sensor to the device"""
        self.sensors.append(sensor)
    
    def collect_data(self):
        """Collect data from all sensors"""
        readings = {"device_id": self.device_id, "timestamp": time.time(), "sensors": []}
        
        for sensor in self.sensors:
            value = sensor.read()
            reading = {"name": sensor.name, "value": value, "unit": sensor.unit}
            readings["sensors"].append(reading)
            print(f"[{self.device_id}] {sensor.name}: {value}{sensor.unit}")
        
        return readings
    
    def process_data(self, data):
        """Process and analyze sensor data"""
        processed = data.copy()
        
        # Check temperature threshold for alert
        for sensor_data in processed["sensors"]:
            if sensor_data["name"] == "Temperature":
                if sensor_data["value"] > 30:
                    sensor_data["alert"] = "High temperature detected!"
                elif sensor_data["value"] < 20:
                    sensor_data["alert"] = "Low temperature detected!"
        
        return processed
    
    def transmit_data(self, data):
        """Simulate data transmission to cloud"""
        payload = json.dumps(data)
        print(f"[Communication] Transmitting: {payload}")
        return True
    
    def enter_sleep_mode(self):
        """Power management - enter low power sleep"""
        self.is_sleeping = True
        print(f"[{self.device_id}] Entering sleep mode for {self.sampling_interval}s...")
        time.sleep(self.sampling_interval)
        self.is_sleeping = False
        print(f"[{self.device_id}] Waking up from sleep")
    
    def run(self, duration=20):
        """Main device operation loop"""
        print(f"=== Starting IoT Device {self.device_id} ===")
        cycle = 0
        
        while cycle < duration:
            # Collect sensor data
            raw_data = self.collect_data()
            
            # Process data locally
            processed_data = self.process_data(raw_data)
            
            # Transmit to network
            self.transmit_data(processed_data)
            
            # Power management
            self.enter_sleep_mode()
            
            cycle += self.sampling_interval
        
        print(f"=== IoT Device {self.device_id} Stopped ===")

# Main execution
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create IoT device (e.g., ESP32-based weather station)
    device = IoTDevice("WEATHER-001", sampling_interval=3)
    
    # Add sensors (Physical Design: Sensors component)
    device.add_sensor(TemperatureSensor())
    device.add_sensor(HumiditySensor())
    
    # Run the device
    device.run(duration=15)
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates the physical components of IoT: sensors (TemperatureSensor, HumiditySensor), the processing unit (IoTDevice class), communication (transmit_data), and power management (enter_sleep_mode). It shows how these components work together in the IoT device architecture.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of sensors per cycle

examples.title: C: Microcontroller Sensor Interface
examples.description: C implementation showing low-level sensor interfacing with a microcontroller (like Arduino/ESP32)
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates low-level IoT device programming typical of microcontrollers like ESP32 or Arduino. It shows sensor reading (ADC input), actuator control (LED, relay), communication module interface, and power management. The code maps directly to the physical components described in the topic: sensors (temperature, humidity, light), actuators (LED, relay), MCU (processing logic), communication (WiFi/BLE), and power management (sleep modes).
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of sensors per cycle

examples.title: Java: IoT Gateway Application
examples.description: Java implementation showing an IoT gateway that aggregates data from multiple sensor nodes
examples.explanation: This Java application demonstrates an IoT gateway collecting data from multiple sensor nodes. It shows device categories (Constrained, Intermediate, Gateway), different communication protocols (MQTT, COAP), data collection, processing, and forwarding to cloud. This represents the gateway device layer in IoT physical design where data from multiple constrained devices is aggregated and sent to the cloud.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of connected nodes
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for node and queue storage
